<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0 confirm this zone is hermetically isolated — E2E tests spawn processes rather than importing production modules, which correctly mirrors the orchestration-tier spawn pattern.
[observation] [info] The breadth of CLI contract tests (arg-contracts, delegation, errors, config, ci, dev) provides strong regression coverage for the orchestration tier, which has no unit tests of its own by design.
[observation] [info] architecture-policy.test.js enforces zone-cycle detection and intra-package layering as a hard gate — this is the correct place for these checks, but any growth in the monorepo should be accompanied by corresponding policy updates in this file.
[pattern] [warning] The domain-isolation.test.js in the E2E zone is the only automated guard against the web-viewer → web-unit boundary violation identified above — verify it explicitly asserts that no production viewer file imports from a test zone, as this class of violation may not be caught by the current gateway-only checks.
[anti-pattern] [warning] architecture-policy.test.js should contain an explicit named allowlist entry for config.js's spawn-exempt exception; without it, the policy test cannot distinguish between the intentional exception and accidental spawn-only violations that happen to access config files.
[suggestion] [warning] The REQUIRED_CONTRACTS gateway coverage check in integration-coverage-policy.test.js matches on package directory name ('hench') rather than gateway path, so one reference to 'hench' in cross-package-contracts.test.js satisfies both the rex-gateway and llm-gateway entries; change the hasReference check to require the gateway file path or a gateway-specific symbol name to make each contract independently verifiable
[suggestion] [info] cli-contract.test.js and cli-arg-contracts.test.js should each carry a one-line comment stating which contract direction they own (cli-contract: orchestration→tool dispatch table; cli-arg-contracts: tool→help-output signatures) to prevent future contributors from treating them as redundant and deleting one

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js act as living enforcement of the four-tier dependency hierarchy — any tier violation causes a CI failure, not just a code review comment
- cli-dev.test.js is marked as a required test in CLAUDE.md (sole dev-mode startup coverage) and must never be skipped or removed
- 22 files with perfect cohesion and zero coupling confirms the suite is fully self-contained — no production zone imports bleed into these tests, keeping the test/source boundary clean
- Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0 confirm this zone is hermetically isolated — E2E tests spawn processes rather than importing production modules, which correctly mirrors the orchestration-tier spawn pattern.
- The breadth of CLI contract tests (arg-contracts, delegation, errors, config, ci, dev) provides strong regression coverage for the orchestration tier, which has no unit tests of its own by design.
- architecture-policy.test.js enforces zone-cycle detection and intra-package layering as a hard gate — this is the correct place for these checks, but any growth in the monorepo should be accompanied by corresponding policy updates in this file.
- The domain-isolation.test.js in the E2E zone is the only automated guard against the web-viewer → web-unit boundary violation identified above — verify it explicitly asserts that no production viewer file imports from a test zone, as this class of violation may not be caught by the current gateway-only checks.
- The architecture-policy.test.js allowlist for config.js (the spawn-exempt exception documented in CLAUDE.md) has not been confirmed to exist as an explicit assertion — if config.js is not explicitly allowlisted, any future orchestration-tier file that breaks spawn-only would pass the policy test if it mimics config.js's access pattern.
- architecture-policy.test.js should contain an explicit named allowlist entry for config.js's spawn-exempt exception; without it, the policy test cannot distinguish between the intentional exception and accidental spawn-only violations that happen to access config files.
- e2e-helpers.js and verify-build.js are non-test utility files counted in the 22-file zone total; integration-coverage-policy.test.js filters by .test.js suffix when computing ratios, so these files do not inflate the proportional-ratio denominator — but sourcevision's zone size metric includes them, making the zone appear larger than its effective test-file count of 20
- The REQUIRED_CONTRACTS list in integration-coverage-policy.test.js verifies gateway coverage by checking whether the gateway's package directory name (e.g. 'hench') appears anywhere in cross-package-contracts.test.js; because both llm-gateway.ts and rex-gateway.ts share the package name 'hench', a single mention of 'hench' in the contract test satisfies both entries simultaneously — the llm-gateway contract requirement is not independently verified
- cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js have distinct and non-overlapping scopes: arg-contracts validates upstream tool CLI help output (what tools accept), while cli-contract validates downstream orchestration invocations (what cli.js and ci.js spawn) — the two-file split is intentional and correct, but the naming does not communicate the inbound/outbound contract direction distinction
- The REQUIRED_CONTRACTS gateway coverage check in integration-coverage-policy.test.js matches on package directory name ('hench') rather than gateway path, so one reference to 'hench' in cross-package-contracts.test.js satisfies both the rex-gateway and llm-gateway entries; change the hasReference check to require the gateway file path or a gateway-specific symbol name to make each contract independently verifiable
- cli-contract.test.js and cli-arg-contracts.test.js should each carry a one-line comment stating which contract direction they own (cli-contract: orchestration→tool dispatch table; cli-arg-contracts: tool→help-output signatures) to prevent future contributors from treating them as redundant and deleting one
- [call graph] 465 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
